title: The Italian Game
summary: Classical, principled, still played at the top

steps.title: The Italian
steps.text:
The Italian Game is one of the **oldest** openings in chess and still — incredibly — one of the most popular at every level, including the top of the world rankings.

## The defining moves

The canonical sequence is {{e4}} {{e5}} {{Nf3}} {{Nc6}} {{Bc4}}. Three principled moves, three pieces developed, and a bishop pointed at f7 — the weakest square in Black's camp.

## What each side wants

- **White's plan:** finish development with {{Nc3}} (or {{c3}} preparing d4), castle short, and slowly build a kingside attack or push d4 to claim the center.
- **Black's plan:** mirror with **...Nf6** and **...Bc5** (the Giuoco Piano) or **...Be7** (the quieter Hungarian), castle short, and contest the center with **...d6** or **...d5**.
- **Pawn structure:** symmetric early, often becoming a closed center after **c3 / d3 / d6**.

## Why it works

The Italian respects every opening principle: a central pawn, two minor pieces out, the king ready to castle. There's no need for hyper-specific theory — just good moves.

> Classical chess at its purest: pieces out, king safe, fight in the center.

steps.prompt: Open with the king's pawn — play **{{e4}}**.
steps.hint: The most popular first move in chess history opens lines for both the queen and the f1 bishop.

steps.prompt: Develop with **{{Nf3}}** — a knight that attacks **e5** and prepares castling.
steps.hint: Two birds with one stone: this move develops a piece AND attacks Black's central pawn.

steps.prompt: Plant the bishop with **{{Bc4}}** — pointing straight at the f7 square.
steps.hint: The most vulnerable square in Black's camp is f7, defended only by the king. A bishop on c4 stares right at it.
